questions.questionText: Tumor suppressor gene p53 is called the 'guardian of the genome' because it:
questions.optionB: Induces cell cycle arrest, DNA repair, or apoptosis in response to DNA damage
questions.explanation: p53 (TP53, chromosome 17p): responds to DNA damage → activates p21 (CDK inhibitor) → G1/S arrest (allows repair). If irreparable → induces apoptosis (via BAX). Most commonly mutated gene in human cancers (~50%). Li-Fraumeni syndrome: germline p53 mutation → multiple cancers (breast, sarcomas, brain tumors, leukemia, adrenal). Loss of both alleles required (two-hit hypothesis). p53 also upregulates MDM2 (negative feedback). Reference: Robbins — p53.

questions.questionText: The most common tumor marker used for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is:
questions.explanation: AFP: elevated in HCC (hepatocellular carcinoma), hepatoblastoma, yolk sac tumor (endodermal sinus tumor), some testicular germ cell tumors. Also elevated in pregnancy (fetal liver production), neural tube defects. CEA: colorectal cancer. CA 19-9: pancreatic cancer. CA-125: ovarian epithelial cancer. PSA: prostate cancer. CA 15-3/CA 27-29: breast cancer. β-hCG: choriocarcinoma, molar pregnancy. Reference: Robbins — Tumor Markers.

questions.questionText: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) BCR-ABL is associated with:
questions.explanation: Philadelphia chromosome: reciprocal translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11) → BCR-ABL fusion gene → constitutively active tyrosine kinase → uncontrolled proliferation. Present in >95% of CML cases (diagnostic). Also in ~25% of adult ALL (poor prognosis). Imatinib (Gleevec): tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting BCR-ABL — revolutionary targeted therapy. Other translocations: t(8;14) c-MYC — Burkitt's, t(14;18) BCL-2 — follicular lymphoma, t(15;17) PML-RARα — APL. Reference: Robbins — CML.

questions.questionText: Psammoma bodies (concentric laminated calcified structures) are characteristically seen in:
questions.optionB: Papillary thyroid carcinoma, papillary serous ovarian cystadenocarcinoma, meningioma
questions.explanation: Psammoma bodies (PSaMMoma mnemonic): Papillary thyroid carcinoma (most commonly tested), Serous papillary cystadenocarcinoma of ovary, Meningioma, Mesothelioma (rarely). These are concentric laminated calcified structures representing dystrophic calcification. NOT seen in follicular or medullary thyroid carcinoma. Other characteristic inclusions: Auer rods (AML-M3), Birbeck granules (Langerhans cell histiocytosis), Call-Exner bodies (granulosa cell tumor). Reference: Robbins — Psammoma Bodies.

questions.optionB: Degree of cellular differentiation and mitotic activity (histological appearance)
questions.explanation: Grading: degree of differentiation (how much tumor resembles the normal tissue of origin). Low grade (well-differentiated, G1): resembles normal tissue, fewer mitoses, better prognosis. High grade (poorly differentiated/undifferentiated, G3-G4): anaplastic, many mitoses, worse prognosis. Staging (TNM): anatomic extent of spread — T (tumor size/invasion), N (lymph node involvement), M (distant metastasis). Staging is generally MORE important than grading for prognosis. Reference: Robbins — Grading vs Staging.

questions.questionText: The most common route of metastasis for carcinomas is:
questions.optionB: Lymphatic spread (to regional lymph nodes first)
questions.explanation: Carcinomas (epithelial tumors): primarily spread via LYMPHATICS initially → regional lymph nodes (sentinel node concept). Sarcomas: primarily spread via HEMATOGENOUS route (bloodstream) → lungs, liver, bone. Exceptions: renal cell carcinoma, HCC, follicular thyroid cancer, choriocarcinoma — early hematogenous spread. Transcoelomic: ovarian cancer (peritoneal seeding), lung cancer (pleural effusion). Perineural invasion: pancreatic cancer, prostate cancer. Reference: Robbins — Metastasis Routes.

questions.questionText: RAS oncogene is most commonly mutated and encodes a:
questions.explanation: RAS (KRAS, HRAS, NRAS): most commonly mutated oncogene family in human cancers (~30%). Encodes small GTPase: signal transduction from growth factor receptors. Normal: active (GTP-bound) → inactive (GDP-bound by GTPase activity). Mutation: locks RAS in active GTP-bound state → constitutive activation of MAPK pathway → uncontrolled proliferation. Mutated in: pancreatic cancer (~90%), colorectal, lung. Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab) only works if KRAS is wild-type. Reference: Robbins — RAS.

questions.questionText: Carcinoma in situ is defined as:
questions.optionB: Pre-invasive neoplasm with full-thickness epithelial dysplasia but NO invasion through the basement membrane
questions.explanation: Carcinoma in situ (CIS): severe dysplasia involving the full thickness of the epithelium but WITHOUT invasion through the basement membrane → no metastatic potential (no access to blood/lymph vessels). Considered the highest grade of pre-invasive neoplasia. Examples: cervical CIS (CIN III), DCIS (breast), CIS of bladder. Once basement membrane is breached → invasive carcinoma (can metastasize). CIS is curable by excision. Reference: Robbins — CIS.

questions.optionB: Substances (hormones, cytokines, antibodies) produced by the tumor that cause systemic effects distant from the tumor
questions.explanation: Paraneoplastic syndromes: systemic effects NOT due to direct invasion or metastasis. Examples: Cushing syndrome (ACTH from small cell lung cancer), SIADH (small cell lung), hypercalcemia (PTHrP — squamous cell lung, renal cell), Lambert-Eaton (anti-VGCC antibodies — small cell lung), acanthosis nigricans (gastric cancer), Trousseau syndrome (migratory thrombophlebitis — pancreatic cancer), polycythemia (EPO — renal cell, HCC). May be first sign of cancer. Reference: Robbins — Paraneoplastic.

questions.questionText: Retinoblastoma gene (Rb) is a tumor suppressor that controls the:
questions.optionB: G1/S checkpoint (when phosphorylated by CDK4/6, releases E2F transcription factor → S phase entry)
questions.explanation: Rb protein: 'governor of G1/S transition.' In its hypophosphorylated state, Rb binds and sequesters E2F transcription factor → blocks S phase genes → cell cycle arrest. Growth signals → cyclin D/CDK4,6 → phosphorylate Rb → releases E2F → S phase progression. Loss of both Rb alleles (two-hit): retinoblastoma. Hereditary (germline + somatic): bilateral retinoblastoma in children. Sporadic: unilateral and later onset. Also lost in osteosarcoma, breast, small cell lung cancer. Reference: Robbins — Rb.

questions.questionText: The Knudson two-hit hypothesis applies to:
questions.optionB: Tumor suppressor gene inactivation (both alleles must be lost for tumor development)
questions.explanation: Knudson two-hit hypothesis: tumor suppressor genes require BOTH alleles to be inactivated (loss of function) for tumor development. Hereditary cancers: first hit is inherited (germline), second hit is somatic → earlier onset, often bilateral/multiple. Sporadic cancers: both hits are somatic → later onset, usually unilateral. Applies to: Rb (retinoblastoma), APC (familial adenomatous polyposis), VHL, WT1, NF1, BRCA1/2. Oncogenes: gain of function, one allele sufficient (dominant). Reference: Robbins — Two-Hit.

questions.questionText: HPV types 16 and 18 are associated with:
questions.optionB: Cervical carcinoma (via E6 inactivation of p53, E7 inactivation of Rb)
questions.explanation: HPV 16,18: high-risk types → cervical squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma. Mechanism: E6 protein → degrades p53 (via ubiquitin-proteasome). E7 protein → inactivates Rb (displaces E2F). Both tumor suppressors lost → uncontrolled proliferation. HPV 6,11: low-risk → genital warts (condylomata acuminata), laryngeal papillomas. HPV vaccination (Gardasil) prevents infection. Pap smear: screening. Other HPV-related cancers: oropharynx, anal, penile, vulvar. Reference: Robbins — HPV Carcinogenesis.

questions.questionText: Teratoma is a germ cell tumor that contains tissues from:
questions.optionB: All three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm)
questions.explanation: Teratoma: germ cell tumor containing differentiated tissues from all 3 germ layers (ectoderm — skin/hair/teeth, mesoderm — bone/muscle/cartilage, endoderm — thyroid/GI epithelium). Mature (benign) teratoma: most common ovarian germ cell tumor in young women (dermoid cyst). Immature teratoma: malignant (contains immature/embryonal tissue). Testicular teratoma: considered malignant (even if mature) in post-pubertal males. Sacrococcygeal teratoma: most common germ cell tumor in children. Reference: Robbins — Teratoma.

questions.questionText: Cachexia in cancer patients is primarily mediated by:
questions.optionB: TNF-α (cachexin), IL-6, and other cytokines causing metabolic derangements
questions.explanation: Cancer cachexia: progressive wasting syndrome (loss of body fat AND skeletal muscle mass) NOT reversed by nutritional supplementation. Mediated by: TNF-α (cachexin — increases lipolysis, inhibits LPL), IL-6, IL-1, IFN-γ, PIF (proteolysis-inducing factor). Pathways: increased basal metabolic rate, increased protein breakdown (ubiquitin-proteasome pathway), lipolysis, insulin resistance. Affects ~50% of cancer patients, accounts for ~20% of cancer deaths. Reference: Robbins — Cancer Cachexia.
